**Glossary of Terms continued**

| Leverage ratio                                                              | To prevent an excessive build-up of leverage on institutions' balance sheets, Basel III introduces a non-risk-based<br>leverage ratio to supplement the risk-based capital framework of Basel II. It is defined as the ratio of tier 1 capital to<br>total exposures. Total exposures include on-balance sheet items, off-balance sheet items and derivatives, and<br>should generally follow the accounting measure of exposure.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Liquidity Coverage<br>Ratio                                                 | Liquidity Coverage Ratio ("LCR"): The ratio of the stock of high quality liquid assets to expected net cash outflows<br>over the next 30 days under a stress scenario. CRD IV requires that this ratio exceeds 100% on 1 January 2018.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| Liquidity risk                                                              | The risk that Group does not have sufficient financial resources to meet its obligations as they fall due, or will have<br>to do so at an excessive cost. This risk arises from mismatches in the timing of cash flows.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Loan to deposit ratio                                                       | This is the ratio of loans and advances expressed as a percentage of customer accounts, as presented in the<br>statement of financial position.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Loan to value                                                               | Loan to value ("LTV") is an arithmetic calculation that expresses the amount of the loan as a percentage of the value<br>of security/collateral. A high LTV indicates that there is less of a cushion to protect the lender against collateral price<br>decreases or increases in the loan carrying amount if repayments are not made and interest is capitalised onto the<br>outstanding loan balance.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Loans past due                                                              | When a borrower fails to make a contractually due payment, a loan is deemed to be past due. 'Past due days'<br>is a term used to describe the cumulative number of days that a missed payment is overdue. Past due days<br>commence from the close of business on the day on which a payment is due but not received. In the case of<br>overdrafts, past due days are counted once a borrower:<br>• has breached an advised limit;<br>• has been advised of a limit lower than the then current amount outstanding; or<br>• has drawn credit without authorisation.<br>When a borrower is past due, the entire exposure is reported as past due, rather than the amount of any excess<br>or arrears. |
| Loss Given Default                                                          | Loss Given Default ("LGD") is the expected or actual loss in the event of default, expressed as a percentage of<br>'exposure at default'.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| Medium term notes                                                           | Medium term notes ("MTNs") are notes issued by the Group across a range of maturities under the European<br>Medium Term Notes ("EMTN") and the Global Medium Term Notes ("GMTN") programmes.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| Minimum requirements<br>for own funds and<br>eligible liabilities<br>(MREL) | A European Union wide requirement under the Bank Recovery and Resolution Directive for all European banks and<br>investment banks to hold a minimum level of equity and/or loss absorbing eligible liabilities to ensure the operation<br>of the bail-in tool to absorb losses and recapitalise an institution in resolution.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| National Asset<br>Management Agency                                         | National Asset Management Agency ("NAMA") was established in 2009 as one of a number of initiatives taken by<br>the Irish Government to address the serious problems which arose in Ireland's banking sector as the result of<br>excessive property lending.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| Net interest income                                                         | The amount of interest received or receivable on assets net of interest paid or payable on liabilities.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Net interest margin                                                         | Net interest margin ("NIM") is a measure of the difference between the interest income generated on average<br>interest earning financial assets (lendings) and the amount of interest paid on average interest bearing financial<br>liabilities (borrowings) relative to the amount of interest-earning assets.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| Net Stable Funding<br>Ratio                                                 | Net Stable Funding Ratio ("NSFR"): The ratio of available stable funding to required stable funding over a 1 year<br>time horizon.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| New transaction<br>lendings                                                 | New transaction lending is defined as incremental increase in drawn balances against facilities granted for a specific<br>period of time whereby the borrower can draw down or repay amounts as required to manage cash flow. It includes<br>revolving credit facilities, overdrafts and invoice discounting facilities.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| Non-performing<br>exposures                                                 | Non-performing exposures are defined by the European Banking Authority to include material exposures which are<br>more than 90 days past due (regardless of whether they are credit impaired) and/or exposures in respect of which<br>the debtor is assessed as unlikely to pay its credit obligations in full without realisation of collateral, regardless of the<br>existence of any past due amount or the number of days the exposure is past due.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Off-balance sheet items                                                     | Off-balance sheet items include undrawn commitments to lend, guarantees, letters of credit, acceptances and other<br>items as listed in Annex I of the CRR.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
|                                                                             |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Offsetting                                                                  | Offsetting, or 'netting', is the presentation of the net amounts of financial assets and financial liabilities in the<br>statement of financial position as a result of Group's rights of set-off.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| Operational risk                                                            | Operational risk is the risk of loss resulting from inadequate or failed internal processes, people and systems or<br>from external events. It includes legal risk, but excludes strategic and business risk. In essence, operational risk is<br>a broad canvas of individual risk types which include product and change risk, outsourcing, information security,<br>cyber, business continuity, health and safety risks, people risk and legal risk.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| Private equity<br>investments                                               | Equity securities in operating companies not quoted on a public exchange, often involving the investment of capital<br>in private companies.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| Probability of Default                                                      | Probability of Default ("PD") is the likelihood that a borrower will default on an obligation to repay.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Regulatory capital                                                          | Regulatory capital is determined in accordance with rules established by the SSM/ECB for the consolidated Group<br>and by local regulators for individual Group companies.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| Re-pricing risk                                                             | Re-pricing risk is a form of interest rate risk (i.e. a type of market risk) that occurs when asset and liability positions<br>are mismatched in terms of re-pricing (as opposed to final contractual) maturity. Where these interest rate gaps are<br>left unhedged, it can result in losses arising in the Group's portfolio of financial instruments.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| Repurchase agreement                                                        | Repurchase agreement ("Repo") is a short-term funding agreement that allows a borrower to create a collateralised<br>loan by selling a financial asset to a lender. As part of the agreement, the borrower commits to repurchase the<br>security at a date in the future repaying the proceeds of the loan. For the counterparty to the transaction, it is termed<br>a reverse repurchase agreement or a reverse repo.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| Residential mortgage-<br>backed securities                                  | Residential mortgage-backed securities ("RMBS") are debt obligations that represent claims to the cash flows from<br>pools of mortgage loans, most commonly on residential property.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| Risk weighted assets                                                        | Risk weighted assets ("RWAs") are a measure of assets (including off-balance sheet items converted into asset<br>equivalents e.g.credit lines) which are weighted in accordance with prescribed rules and formulas as defined in the<br>Basel Accord to reflect the risks inherent in those assets.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| Securities financing<br>transactions                                        | Securities financing transactions allow investors and firms to use assets, such as the shares or bonds they own,<br>to secure funding for their activities.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| Securitisation                                                              | Securitisation is the process of aggregation and repackaging of non-tradable financial instruments such as loans and<br>advances, or company cash flows into securities that can be issued and traded in the capital markets.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| Single Resolution Fund                                                      | The Single Resolution Fund (SRF) is an emergency fund that can be called upon in times of crisis.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| Single Supervisory<br>Mechanism                                             | The Single Supervisory Mechanism ("SSM") is a system of financial supervision comprising the European Central<br>Bank ("ECB") and the national competent authorities of participating EU countries. The main aims of the SSM are to<br>ensure the safety and soundness of the European banking system and to increase financial integration and stability<br>in Europe.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Special purpose entity                                                      | Special purpose entity ("SPE") is a legal entity which can be a limited company or a limited partnership created to<br>fulfil narrow or specific objectives. A company will transfer assets to the SPE for management or use by the SPE to<br>finance a large project thereby achieving a narrow set of goals without putting the entire firm at risk. This term is<br>used interchangeably with SPV (special purpose vehicle).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |

# **Glossary of Terms continued**

| Stage allocation:                               | Under IFRS 9, loans and advances to customers, other than POCIs are classified into one of three stages:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
|-------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Stage 1                                         | Includes newly originated loans and loans that have not had a significant increase in credit risk since<br>initial recognition.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| Stage 2                                         | Includes loans that have had a significant increase in credit risk since initial recognition but do not have objective<br>evidence of being credit impaired.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| Stage 3                                         | Includes loans that are defaulted or are otherwise considered to be credit impaired.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| POCI                                            | POCIs are assets originated credit impaired and that have a discount to the contractual value when measured at fair<br>value.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| Stress testing                                  | Stress testing is a technique used to evaluate the potential effects on an institution's financial condition of an<br>exceptional but plausible event and/or movement in a set of financial variables.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Structured securities                           | This involves non-standard lending arrangements through the structuring of assets or debt issues in accordance<br>with customer and/or market requirements. The requirements may be concerned with funding, liquidity, risk transfer<br>or other needs that cannot be met by an existing off the shelf product or instrument. To meet this requirement,<br>existing products and techniques must be engineered into a tailor-made product or process.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| Subordinated liabilities                        | Liabilities which, in the event of insolvency or liquidation of the issuer, are subordinated to the claims of depositors<br>and other creditors of the issuer.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Syndicated and<br>international lending         | Syndicated and international lending involves lending to entities by leveraging off their equity structures having<br>considered the cash generating capacity of the business and its capacity to repay any associated debt. Leveraging<br>structures are typically used in management and private equity buy-outs, mergers and acquisitions. Syndicated<br>and international lending is extended typically to non-investment grade borrowers and carries commensurate rates<br>of return.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| Tier 1 capital                                  | A measure of a bank's financial strength defined by the Basel Accord. It captures common equity tier 1 capital and<br>other instruments in issue that meet the criteria for inclusion as additional tier 1 capital. These are subject to certain<br>regulatory deductions.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| Tier 2 capital                                  | Broadly includes qualifying subordinated debt and other tier 2 securities in issue. It is subject to adjustments relating<br>to the excess of expected loss on the IRBA portfolios over the accounting expected credit losses on the IRBA<br>portfolios, securitisation positions and material holdings in financial companies.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| Tracker mortgage                                | A mortgage with a variable interest rate which tracks the European Central Bank ("ECB") rate, at an agreed margin<br>above the ECB rate and will increase or decrease within five days of an ECB rate movement.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| Trade date and<br>settlement date<br>accounting | 1. Trade date accounting records the transaction on the date on which an agreement has been entered (the trade<br>date), instead of on the date the transaction has been finalised (the settlement date).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
|                                                 | 2. Under the settlement date accounting approach, the asset is recognised on the date on which it is received by<br>the Group, on disposal, the asset is not derecognised until the asset is delivered to the buyer.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| Value at Risk                                   | The Group's core risk measurement methodology is based on an historical simulation application of the industry<br>standard Value at Risk ("VaR") technique. The methodology incorporates the portfolio diversification effect within<br>each standard risk factor (interest rate, credit spread, foreign exchange, equity, as applicable). The resulting VaR<br>figures, calculated at the close of business each day, are an estimate of the probable maximum loss in fair value<br>over a one day holding period that would arise from an adverse movement in market rates. This VaR metric is<br>derived from an observation of historical prices over a period of one year and assessed at a 95% statistical<br>confidence level (i.e. the VaR metric may be exceeded at least 5% of the time). |
| Wholesale funding                               | Wholesale funding refers to funds raised from wholesale market sources. Examples of wholesale funding include<br>senior unsecured bonds, covered bonds, securitisations, repurchase transactions, interbank deposits and deposits<br>raised from non-bank financial institutions.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| Yield curve risk                                | A type of market risk that refers to the possibility that an interest rate yield curve changes its shape unexpectedly<br>(e.g. flattening, steepening, non-parallel shift), resulting in losses arising in the Group's portfolio of interest<br>rate instruments.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
